# **What is geodesy?**

## Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding the Earth's **geometric shape**, **orientation in space**, and **gravity field**.

![Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding three fundamental properites of the Earth](/facts/geodesy.jpg)

*Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding three fundamental properites of the Earth.*

Many organizations use geodesy to map the U.S. shoreline, determine land boundaries, and improve transportation and navigation safety. To measure points on the Earth’s surface, geodesists assign coordinates (similar to a unique address) to points all over the Earth. In the past, geodesists determined the coordinates of points by using Earth-based surveying tools to measure the distances between points. Today, geodesists use space-based tools like the Global Positioning System (GPS) to measure points on the Earth’s surface.

Geodesists must accurately define the coordinates of points on the surface of the Earth in a consistent manner. A set of accurately measured points is the basis for the National Spatial Reference System, which allows different kinds of maps to be consistent with one another.

To measure the Earth, geodesists build simple mathematical models of the Earth which capture the largest, most obvious features. Geodesists have adopted the ellipsoid as the most basic model of the Earth. Because the ellipsoid is based on a very simple mathematical model, it can be completely smooth and does not include any mountains or valleys. When additional detail of the Earth is needed, geodesists use the [geoid](/facts/geoid.html). A geoid has a shape very similar to global mean sea level, but this exists over the whole globe, not just over the oceans.

**Geodesy** or **geodetics**[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-1) is the [science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science "Science") of measuring and representing the [geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth"), [gravity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravity_of_Earth "Gravity of Earth"), and [spatial orientation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_rotation "Earth's rotation") of the [Earth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth "Earth") in [temporally varying](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_change "Relative change")[3D](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space"). It is called [planetary geodesy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planetary_geodesy "Planetary geodesy") when studying other [astronomical bodies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Astronomical_body "Astronomical body"), such as [planets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planet "Planet") or [circumplanetary systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-VK-2)

[Geodynamical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodynamics "Geodynamics") phenomena, including [crustal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crust_(geology) "Crust (geology)") motion, [tides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tide "Tide"), and [polar motion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar_motion "Polar motion"), can be studied by designing global and national [control networks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodetic_control_network "Geodetic control network"), applying [space geodesy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_geodesy "Space geodesy") and terrestrial geodetic techniques, and relying on [datums](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodetic_datum "Geodetic datum") and [coordinate systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system").

Geodetic job titles include **geodesist** and **geodetic surveyor**.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geodesy#cite_note-3)

# Geodesy: The Invisible Backbone of Navigation

## NOAA Ocean Podcast: Episode 12

### *Geodesy is a science that covers the Earth itself. It's the reason we know where we are, and how we can make maps of where we are and where we want to go. In this episode, learn how this science with an unfamiliar name shapes much of what we do every day.*

![Earth from Space](/podcast/jan18/geodesy.jpg "While we often think of the Earth as a sphere, our planet is actually very bumpy and irregular. ")

While we often think of the Earth as a sphere, our planet is actually very bumpy and irregular. Geodesy is the science of accurately measuring and understanding the Earth's geometric shape, orientation in space, and gravity field. This composite color full-disk visible image was captured on January 15, 2017, by GOES-16, the first spacecraft in NOAAâs next-generation of geostationary satellites.

### Transcript

HOST: Youâre listening to the NOAA Ocean podcastâ¦ Iâm Megan Forbes. In this
episode weâre going to talk about a science that effects our everyday
lives, but that you might not be familiar with. In fact, I would venture to

Geodesy is the science that accurately measures and understands our
planetâs geometric shape, its orientation in space, and its field of
gravity. All of these things have an important effect on our lives, but are
always working the background where most of us donât notice. Geodesy is so
important that NOAA has an entire program offices dedicated to geodetic
information. I wanted to learn more about geodesy, so I sat down with Galen
Scott with the Geosciences Research Division of the National Geodetic
Survey, and we talked about the basics of this science and why it is such a
fascinating subject.

HOST: Thanks, Galen, for meeting me here today at the Collaboration Center
in the NOAA Campus in Silver Spring. So why donât you walk us through a
little bit about geodesy. I have to say, before I came to NOAA, I had never
heard the word *geodesy*, although I understood time and space and
gravity separately, I certainly didnât know of the science that puts that
all together. Even after working here, I still had to do some research on
it â so I thought, âwhy not talk to somebody who knows a lot about itâ so
you can explain it to me and hopefully to any of our other listeners out
there.

GALEN SCOTT: Great! Well, thanks for having me, Iâm really glad to be
talking to you today. Geodesy is the study of the size and shape of the
Earth and how we position ourselves upon it. It also has to do with the
study of gravity and the variations of gravity across the planetâ¦because
that has implications for the instruments that we use to do surveying, it
has implications for which way water flows. Itâs a science that covers the
Earth itself, how we know where we are, and how we can make maps of where
we are and where we want to go. If a person is considering just the town or
the village where they are, [they] can essentially think of the Earth as a
flat surface, because theyâre only going relatively small distances. Once
you start going farther and farther away, you need to start accounting for
the fact that the Earth is curved. The Earth curves at about 8 inches per
mile.

HOST: So how would you describe the shape of the Earthâ¦because thatâs
really important to understanding geodesy, right?

GALEN SCOTT: Right. So the Earth is what we call an oblate spheroid. Itâs a
sphere, but itâs kind of squished and it is fatter in the middle at the
equator than it is at the poles. Itâs actually rather âlumpy and
bumpyââ¦itâs not a perfect sphere. Being able to measure that and monitor
that requires being able to basically see it from out in space. If you
think about navigation â what this really started with was navigation and
the questions of âhow do I get there?â, âwhich direction do I need to go to
get from one place to another?â and âhow far is it?â- all of those
questions require some geodesy. We have to account for that curvature of
the Earth, so making a map of a large area gets to be a little more complex
than making a map of a small town. Geodesy is about measuring angles and
distances. We started off looking at astronomic angles and distances to the
stars to support navigation. Historically, we used the stars for
navigation, and the orientation of ourselves on the Earth relative to the
sun and the moon and the stars was really what helped people explore the
Earth. Back in the day before GPS, people figured out, if they knew they
wanted to be going north, they would check the length of their shadow each
day at noon, and at night, they could track their progress by the
orientation of the North Star. So this relationship between the Earth and
the stars really provides those tools for those everyday applications of
geodesy.

HOST: Why do you think we know so little about geodesy?

GALEN SCOTT: Geodesy is one of the Earth sciences â those sciences
identified by âgeoâ at the beginning of the word â geography, geologyâ¦
Geodesy comes from Greek, meaning âto segment the Earthâ, to divide it up
into segments that we can then understand a little better and measure.
Geodesy is a combination of different sciences, particularly physics and
mathematics. Thereâs a lot of things to consider when youâre thinking about
the Earth and the gravity field, and the Earthâs orientation in space, and
so one of the reasons why geodesy is relatively unknown is because it is
quite complex and it is a combination of other quite complex sciences.

Soâ¦gravity is a function of mass. The more massive an object is, the more
gravity it has and the stronger the pull of gravity is towards that. For
example, a large mountain has a stronger attraction of gravity than a
nearby valley because thereâs more mass in that mountain. We actually
measure the strength of gravity to create a model of the Earthâs gravity
field call a geoid model. The geoid model is a model of what the Earth
would look like if the entire Earth was: covered in water, there were no
waves or tides or currents, and the water would seek its own level relative
to gravity. It would be a smooth undulating surface with the water being
higher in areas where the gravity value is higher, and dipping down where
the gravity values are lower.

HOST: Thereâs a free plan on the National Ocean Service website where you
can actually print your own scale model of a geoid. It demonstrates a
little more simply what youâve just talked about. You can check it out on
the Ocean Service website.

GALEN SCOTT: Yeah, thatâs a really cool little project. The geoid model is
what we use to measure surface elevations with a high degree of accuracy.

HOST: Thinking about crossing our countryâ¦using geodesy to create the
railway system or the road system that we use to cart things back and forth
across the countryâ¦ships to navigate inâ¦geodesy is such an important part
of our economy and our consumer products that we probably wouldnât do very
well without it!

GALEN SCOTT: So when we started to build structuresâ¦homes and roads and
bridges and tunnelsâ¦and particularly things like aqueducts and sewers and
plumbing, it was really important for us to know which way water flows. So
we really had to consider gravity and the idea that geodesy can help us
measure heights very precisely and design things like water control
structures. Thatâs a really crucial piece of all this, is that this is a
science that helps us understand Earthâs systems and how things move, and
gives us the ability to make measurements that can allow us to build these
big structures. If we werenât able to measure heights accurately, we
wouldnât be able to really be confident that the sewer pipe is going to run *away* from where weâre living (laughter). The other piece of it is
about property ownership. When we have questions of âwhat is my property?â,
âwhere is your property?â, âwhere is the line?ââ¦surveying geodesy is what
helps us to be able to make those determinations. That has a very important
role in terms of property ownership, but also in terms of taxes â for tax
purposes and the governmentâs understanding of where different plots of
lands are and how they relate to each other so that they can be taxed
appropriately.

If you think about itâ¦once you start looking at large products like
building a railway across the country or the interstate highway system, you
need to have something that accounts for the shape of the Earth over those
larger distances. Surveyors, to measure points on the Earth, assign
coordinates â like a unique address for any point on the Earth â so that we
can build whatever weâre trying to build. For something like railway or
interstate highway system, we need to be able to use the same consistent
coordinate system so that things line up appropriately. Imagine if we had
two different coordinate systems and we wanted to build a highway from one
state to another, and we started to build from either end toward the
middleâ¦well, if you use two different coordinate systems, thereâs a good
chance you are not going to meet cleanly in the middle. So, having that
consistent coordinate system for the whole country allows us to do things
like build a road starting at two different ends and be certain that we
will meet in the middle, because we have that accurate addressing system
for those places. So that concept of having a consistent national
coordinate system is what my office is responsible for. The National
Geodetic Survey is responsible for what is called the National Spatial
Reference System. That Reference system provides that foundational
reference frame for all kinds of geospatial activity, to make sure weâre
all starting at the same zero point, at the same coordinates, and we can do
this building and this construction and be confident that things will meet
in the middle where they are supposed to.

HOST: So you talked about the National Spatial Reference Systemâ¦how does
geodesy translate between countries?

GALEN SCOTT: The National Spatial Reference System takes part of what is
called the International Terrestrial Reference Frame, that works on a
global scale and is a best-fit for the globe as a whole, and we kind of
massage that and move it and change it around a little so that it is more
accurate for the U.S. The mapping system in the U.S. is defined by our
Federal agency, but other agencies and other countries around the world
create their own reference systems for better maps for their particular
places. Think aboutâ¦your cell phone and wanting to use Google Maps on your
cell phone. That map is putting together data from many, many different
sources. You have data about the buildings, and the restaurants, and the
office buildings and your home, and where those buildings are in relation
to each otherâ¦and then youâve got the road networks and the metro system
and other layers of information that you need to be able to navigate and to
do the things that you do every day.

HOST: So, really, if you are planning to move yourself anywhere â whether
itâs walking or driving, going cross-town or within states, you are really
using geodesy whether you know it or not!

GALEN SCOTT: Right! Geodesy â you can think of it as âthe infrastructure of
infrastructureâ. It is that invisible backbone of science and technology
behind all of this data that your phone is putting together that enables it
all to line up and work well together. Itâs one of those things that is
pretty much invisible to youâ¦until it doesnât work.

HOST: Right.

GALEN SCOTT: So one of the things that we are doing right now is we are
creating a new reference frame. Coming up in 2022 we will be replacing all
of the datums, the pieces of the National Spatial Reference System, to
create a new set of reference frames for the country.

HOST: Wow!

GALEN SCOTT: Yeah, and itâs one of my favorite acronyms! The big project
that is pushing us toward that goal is called GRAV-D, or Gravity for the
Redefinition of the American Vertical Datum. GRAV-D is going to create a
new model of gravity, a new geoid modelâ¦a much more accurate and precise
geoid model. Weâre going to be able to use that geoid model with the
information that comes from your GPS receivers to get a much more precise
and accurate position and height on the Earthâs surface. The really big
benefit of this is to be able to much better map and model which way water
flows, particularly in flat areas.

HOST: Iâd like to know what personally makes geodesy such a cool subject
for you â someone who thinks about it a lot more often than I do.

GALEN SCOTT: What I really love about geodesy is that itâs so
interdisciplinary! There are so many different types of science that go
into it and it can answer so many different questions. There are so many
different applications to it and it is important in so many different ways
that people just really donât understand. So this idea of using different
pieces of science, different bits of science from different disciplines and
putting them all together is really what interests me most about it. The
basic questions of âwhere are we?â, âwhere do we want to go?â, and âhow do
we get there?â are fundamental questions weâve asked for eons and weâre
even asking todayâ¦and geodesy gives us a way to answer those questions.

HOST: Thatâs it for this episode of the NOAA Ocean Podcast. Thanks to Galen
Scott for helping us understand the science of geodesy and its importance
in our lives. To learn more about this science, or any ocean-related topic,
visit our website at oceanservice.noaa.gov. We appreciate you taking the
time to learn with us, and hope youâll join us again soon. Until
thenâ¦thanks for listening.

# Exploring Geodesy

### The Science of Measuring Our Planet

# What is Geodesy?

*The Earth is a restless planet. It is subject to a wide range of external and internal processes that change its shape (including [continental](https://geodesy.science/item/dem-digital-elevation-model/), [oceanic](https://geodesy.science/item/ocean-topography-models/) and [ice-covered](https://geodesy.science/item/ice-sheets-glaciers/) areas), the [distribution of mass](https://geodesy.science/item/global-gravity-field-models/) between and within its components (geosphere, biosphere, cryosphere, hydrosphere and atmosphere), and its [rotation and orientation in space](https://geodesy.science/item/earth-orientation-parameter/). **The task of geodesy is to***

***determine the size, shape, gravity field and rotation of the Earth***

***as a function of space and time.** These properties react together, albeit in different ways, to processes in each of the components of the Earth system. Therefore, in order to achieve its primary objective, geodesy is necessarily involved in the detection, analysis and modelling of the signals emitted by the Earth system, in particular those recorded by geodetic observations. In this way, geodesy is able to monitor the Earth system and measure global change, serving science and society far beyond the traditional task of measuring and mapping the Earth’s surface.*

#### [Geodetic Observations](https://geodesy.science/ggos/obs/)

![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/ObservationsImage.svg "ObservationsImage")

#### [Geodetic Products](https://geodesy.science/ggos/products/)

![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/ProductsImage.svg "ProductsImage")

*Geodesy involves measuring and understanding the Earth’s geometric and physical properties, including*

*Geodesy is essential for satellite positioning applications such as [GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite Systems)](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/), observation of the Earth from [satellites](https://geodesy.science/ggos/obs/), land management and [climate studies](https://geodesy.science/iccc/). It uses [space and ground-based technologies](https://geodesy.science/ggos/obs/) to ensure accurate measurements.*

# Geodesy in Everyday Life

### Empowering Society and Shaping Our Future

## **Framing our World**

#### Providing the Basis not only for Positioning, Navigation, and Timing but also for Earth System Monitoring

*Imagine trying to navigate on a changing planet without a [stable frame of reference](https://geodesy.science/item/terrestrial-reference-frame/) — it would be like trying to measure the displacement of one moving object from another moving object. Geodetic reference frames, such as the [International Terrestrial Reference Frame (ITRF)](https://geodesy.science/item/terrestrial-reference-frame/), provide the invisible but essential foundation for everything from [GNSS navigation](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/) and satellite-based Earth observation to climate monitoring and disaster management. Recognized by the United Nations as a key tool for sustainable development, these reference frames allow scientists to detect even the [tiniest shifts in the Earth’s surface](https://geodesy.science/item/station-positions-variations/), help governments coordinate consistent geographic data across borders, and tell you where you are when you ask your mobile phone. Without them, modern life and science would be unthinkable.*

***[Would you like to know how we can track a planet in motion?](https://geodesy.science/com1/)***

##### Find out how much your daily life depends on reference frames!

[long version available here (in several languages)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vvNXv05646M&list=PLIIfsJS7iAux5FEFB4tkVkdV__MJRhACc&index=2)

##### What would a day without satellites look like?

## **Shaping the Path**

#### Geodesy’s Central Role in Navigation and Mobility

*From autonomous vehicles to aircraft navigation and the smartphone in your pocket, modern mobility depends on geodesy. [Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/), such as GPS, GLONASS, GALILEO and BDS, rely on [precise geodetic reference frames](https://geodesy.science/item/terrestrial-reference-frame/) to determine [position](https://geodesy.science/item/station-positions-variations/) anywhere on Earth and [in space](https://geodesy.science/item/celestial-reference-frame/) at any time. This geodetic backbone enables, among many others, safe air travel, guides rescue teams during disasters, and powers location-based services used by billions of people every day. For researchers it is the main tool for detecting and understanding changes in the Earth system; for society, it is the hidden foundation for infrastructure development and helps us move through the world with confidence.*

[***Curious how geodesy keeps us on track?***](https://geodesy.science/com4/)

## **Saving Lives**

#### Geodesy in Disaster Management and Risk Reduction

*When every second counts, geodesy can make the difference between safety and catastrophe. By enhancing **early warning systems** for **earthquakes**, **tsunamis**, and **volcanic eruptions**, geodetic technologies such as [GNSS](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/) are helping to save lives. For example, [GNSS-based Tsunami Early Warning Systems (GTEWS)](https://geodesy.science/ggos/structure/fa/geohazards/) can detect [atmospheric disturbances](https://geodesy.science/item/atmospheric-products/) caused by tsunamis in real time, providing valuable minutes of warning. But the role of geodesy goes even further — satellite geodetic data help monitor [land subsidence](https://geodesy.science/item/surface-deformation-models/), [glacier collapse](https://geodesy.science/item/ice-sheets-glaciers/), and [sea level rise](https://geodesy.science/item/sea-level-change/), all of which are critical to understand long-term risks. Governments and emergency services depend on this data to improve preparedness, create evacuation plans, and build resilient infrastructure. In a world of increasing environmental hazards, **geodesy is a silent but powerful ally in protecting lives and livelihoods**.*

[***Want to see how it works on the front lines of disaster response?***](https://geodesy.science/ggos/structure/fa/geohazards/)

![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/AdobeStock_501769733-scaled-1-1024x681.jpeg "Devastation concept. Waves of high tide created by a tsunami des")
![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/urban_cityAdobeStock_5248625491-1024x590.jpeg "urban_cityAdobeStock_524862549(1)")

## **Building the Future**

#### Geodesy in Urban Planning and Infrastructure

*Modern cities need solid foundations — and not just in concrete. Geodesy provides the [precise position](https://geodesy.science/item/station-positions-variations/) and [elevation data](https://geodesy.science/item/height-systems/) that urban planners and engineers rely on to design infrastructure, manage land and resources efficiently, monitor deformation, and ensure structural safety. From tunnels and bridges to high-speed rail and smart cities, **geodetic measurements support long-term stability and resilience**. Accurate geospatial data helps policymakers make informed decisions about land use, risk zones, and sustainable development. As climate change and rapid urbanization reshape our world, geodesy ensures that the cities of tomorrow are not only innovative, but also safe, efficient, and durable.*

[***Are you curious about how geodesy shapes the spaces we live in?***](https://geodesy.science/com4/)

## **Keeping Time**

#### The Crucial Link Between Geodesy and Timekeeping

*Time may seem constant, but even our clocks depend on the changes of our dynamic planet — and that is where geodesy comes in. The [rotation of the Earth](https://geodesy.science/item/earth-orientation-parameter/) and even [tiny shifts in its mass](https://geodesy.science/item/gravity-field-temporal-variations/) all affect how we define and keep time. Geodesy helps to synchronize atomic clocks with the actual motion of the planet, ensuring the accuracy of global time systems such as the [Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)](https://geodesy.science/item/earth-orientation-parameter/). This precision is essential for [satellite navigation](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/), telecommunications, financial transactions, and scientific research. **Without geodesy, our modern timekeeping would drift — literally**.*

[***Do you want to know how measuring Earth keeps the world on time?***](https://geodesy.science/item/iers/)

![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/clock-time-AdobeStock_2102140981-1024x684.jpeg "clock-time-AdobeStock_210214098(1)")

# The Earth

### Geodesy and the Precise Measurement of Our Planet

![Whole Earth Image](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/EarthSurface_stockio-scaled-1-1024x512.jpeg "EarthSurface_stockio")

## **Earth’s Surface**

#### The Exact Geometric Form of our Globe?

*Where does the land begin and the sea end? How high is a mountain, how deep are the oceans? **Geodesy provides the precise measurements needed to model the Earth’s surface in detail** — its [shape](https://geodesy.science/item/dem-digital-elevation-model/), [elevation](https://geodesy.science/item/height-systems/), and [features](https://geodesy.science/ggos/products/). This knowledge forms the foundation for accurate maps, national borders, infrastructure planning and management, and environmental monitoring. Scientists use geodetic surface models (on [land](https://geodesy.science/item/dem-digital-elevation-model/) and [sea](https://geodesy.science/item/sea-surface-heights/)) to study [sea level rise](https://geodesy.science/item/sea-level-change/), [landslides](https://geodesy.science/item/surface-deformation-models/), and natural hazards, while governments rely on them to manage land resources and respond to climate challenges. Whether it is guiding global navigation or informing local development, **geodesy ensures that we really know the ground we are standing on**.*

[***Curious about how we transform the complex shape of Earth into actionable knowledge?***](https://geodesy.science/ggos/products/)

## **Earth’s Gravity**

#### The Force Shaping our Planet

*Gravity is much more than a force that keeps us on the ground — it is critical to understanding the structure and changes of the Earth system. Geodesy measures [gravity variations](https://geodesy.science/item/gravity-field-temporal-variations/) to construct detailed [models of the Earth’s gravity field](https://geodesy.science/item/global-gravity-field-models/), revealing the distribution and transport of mass beneath the surface. These gravity models are essential for accurate [height determination](https://geodesy.science/item/height-systems/), allowing us to measure precise elevations and topographies, from mountain peaks to ocean floors, but also the [height of the ocean surface](https://geodesy.science/item/sea-surface-heights/). Temporal variations in the gravity field, such as those caused by [melting ice](https://geodesy.science/item/ice-sheets-glaciers/) or [shifting tectonic plates](https://geodesy.science/item/surface-deformation-models/), provide valuable insights into global processes such as [climate change](https://geodesy.science/iccc/) and mass transport between and within the different components of the Earth system. By combining [satellite gravimetry](https://geodesy.science/item/satellite-gravimetry/) with [terrestrial gravity](https://geodesy.science/item/terrestrial-gravimetry/) data, scientists can better understand phenomena such as [sea level rise](https://geodesy.science/item/sea-level-change/), ice sheet dynamics, and [gravity anomalies](https://geodesy.science/item/regional-geoid-models/). The study of gravity is key to monitoring the Earth’s motion, enhancing our knowledge of its evolution, and supporting applications in navigation, climate science, and environmental protection.*

[***Curious how gravity shapes the world we live in?***](https://geodesy.science/com2/)

![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/gravity-apple-pexels-cottonbro-46612941-972x1024.jpg "gravity-apple-pexels-cottonbro-4661294(1)")
![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/glacier-ice-melting-cliamte-change-AdobeStock_1819199071-1024x683.jpeg "glacier-ice-melting-cliamte-change-AdobeStock_181919907(1)")

## **Earth in Motion**

#### Monitoring a Changing Planet

*Our planet is constantly moving — [tectonic plates shift](https://geodesy.science/item/station-positions-variations/), [glaciers melt](https://geodesy.science/item/ice-sheets-glaciers/), [sea level rise](https://geodesy.science/item/sea-level-change/), and [gravity](https://geodesy.science/item/gravity-field-temporal-variations/) itself changes over time. **Geodesy allows us to measure, monitor and understand these dynamic processes with millimeter precision.** By monitoring crustal movements, earthquake effects, and volcanic activity, geodesy contributes to risk reduction and geological/geophysical understanding. At the same time, [satellite-based geodetic measurements](https://geodesy.science/ggos/obs/) are vital for tracking climate change, showing how [ice sheets shrink](https://geodesy.science/item/ice-sheets-glaciers/), [oceans expand](https://geodesy.science/item/ocean-topography-models/), and the [Earth’s mass redistributes](https://geodesy.science/item/gravity-field-temporal-variations/). This data is essential for environmental policy, international climate agreements, and scientific models of future change. At a time of global transformation, geodesy gives us the tools to understand, adapt, and protect our planet.*

[**Would you like to learn how geodetic observations enable continuous monitoring of our planet?**](https://geodesy.science/ggos/obs/)

# The Earth in Space

### Geodesy’s Role Beyond Earth

## **Observation from Space**

#### Quantifying changes in the Earth System through Satellites

*The Earth is under constant observation by an increasing number of satellites orbiting our planet, forming a network of space-based observatories that provide insight “from above” into the movements and transformations of our planet. Dedicated satellite missions monitor different components of the Earth system: [atmosphere](https://geodesy.science/item/atmospheric-products/) (air), [hydrosphere](https://geodesy.science/item/satellite-gravimetry/) (water), [cryosphere](https://geodesy.science/item/satellite-gravimetry/) (frozen water), [lithosphere](https://geodesy.science/item/satellite-altimetry/) (solid Earth) and biosphere (living beings), providing critical data on climate change, geophysical processes and human-induced transformations. Geodesy is responsible for the accurate determination of [satellite orbits](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss-orbits-clocks/), which in turn are essential for reliable measurements of the changing Earth.*

[***Want to know why satellite-based Earth observation would not be possible without geodesy?***](https://geodesy.science/ggos/obs/)

![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/gravity-field-earth-AdobeStock_5354814821-1024x429.jpeg "gravity-field-earth-AdobeStock_535481482(1)")
![](https://geodesy.science/wp-content/uploads/stars-rotation-earth-AdobeStock_115831905-1024x744.jpeg "Firmamento - Startrail - cielo di notte")

## **The Rotation of the Earth**

#### How Geodesy Tracks the Rotation of our Planet

*Why are the days getting longer, and why is the Earth wobbling? Geodesy answers these questions by monitoring the Earth’s rotation through [Earth Orientation Parameters (EOPs)](https://geodesy.science/item/earth-orientation-parameter/). These parameters track shifts in the Earth’s axis and orientation, which are influenced by the atmosphere, oceans, and solid Earth. Using geodetic techniques such as [GNSS](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/), [VLBI](https://geodesy.science/item/vlbi/), [SLR](https://geodesy.science/item/slr-llr/), and [DORIS](https://geodesy.science/item/doris/), geodesy measures these changes with precision, supporting [satellite navigation](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss/), [orbit determination](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss-orbits-clocks/), and time systems. The [International Celestial Reference Frame (ICRF)](https://geodesy.science/item/celestial-reference-frame/) links Earth to space by cataloging extragalactic radio sources and providing an accurate celestial reference. This knowledge deepens our understanding of the Earth’s dynamics, including the effects of climate change and geophysical processes on its rotation. Geodesy keeps us in sync with the Earth’s motion, which is crucial for [global reference frames](https://geodesy.science/item/terrestrial-reference-frame/) and [climate research](https://geodesy.science/iccc/).*

[***Curious how these changes are affecting your world?***](https://geodesy.science/com3/)

## **Measuring the Space**

#### Geodesy’s Role in Space Exploration

*Geodesy not only measures the Earth, but also extends its reach into space, playing an essential role in the exploration of the Moon and planets. The [International Celestial Reference Frame (ICRF)](https://geodesy.science/item/celestial-reference-frame/) is a key component in linking Earth-based observations to the wider cosmos. By providing a precise celestial reference frame, the ICRF supports [satellite navigation](https://geodesy.science/item/gnss-radio-occultation/), space missions, and the accurate determination of spacecraft trajectories. As humanity ventures further into space, geodesy ensures that we can navigate and study distant planetary bodies with the same level of precision that we use to map the Earth. For planetary exploration, a **planetary geodetic reference system** is equally important, allowing us to establish accurate coordinates and monitor the surface movements of other bodies such as Mars or the Moon. This is crucial for mission planning, surface exploration, and understanding the geophysical properties of other worlds. Geodesy thus acts as the foundation for our growing understanding of space and the successful navigation of space missions, from Earth to the far reaches of the Solar System and beyond.*

## Science and Applications

Geodesy is the science of the Earth’s shape, gravity and rotation, including their evolution in time. A number of different techniques are used to observe the geodetic properties of the Earth including the space-geodetic techniques of Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI), Satellite Laser Ranging (SLR), Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) like the US Global Positioning System (GPS), and the French Doppler Orbitography and Radio-Positioning by Integrated Satellite (DORIS) system. These space-geodetic observations also provide the basis for the reference frame that is needed in order to assign coordinates to points and objects and thereby determine how those points and objects move over time.

![](/images/science2_300.jpg)

Improving the performance of the Global Geodetic Observing System (GGOS) and the ITRF is critical to understanding the future impact of sea level change. The GGOS geodetic reference frame requirement for sea level measurement is 1 mm reference frame accuracy and 0.1 mm/year stability. From: National Research Council. Precise Geodetic Infrastructure: National Requirements for a Shared Resource. Washington, DC: The National Academies Press, 2010.

The Terrestrial Reference Frame (TRF) is the foundation for virtually all airborne, space-based and ground-based Earth observations. Through its tie to the Celestial Reference Frame (CRF) by time-dependent Earth orientation parameters, it is also fundamentally important for interplanetary spacecraft tracking and navigation. The TRF determined by geodetic measurements is the indispensable foundation for all geo-referenced data used by society. It plays a key role in modeling and estimation of the motion of the Earth in space, in measuring change and deformation of all components of the Earth system, and in providing the ability to connect measurements made at the same places at different times, a critical requirement for understanding global, regional and local change.

Space geodesy has revolutionized the study of solid Earth processes through its ability to measure the deformation of the Earth’s surface and the Earth’s gravity field with extraordinary accuracy. These provide information about the motion of the Earth’s tectonic plates, insight into the cause and timing of earthquakes and volcanic eruptions, and constraints on the internal forces that drive them. Understanding the complex Earth system requires integrated and geo-referenced sets of observations on global to regional spatial scales and with high spatial and temporal resolution.

Modern geodetic measurements are making fundamental contributions to mitigating the impact of geohazards such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, debris flows, landslides, land subsidence, sea level change, tsunamis, floods, storm surges, hurricanes and extreme weather. The ability to relate measurements to one another in both space and time depends on knowledge of the terrestrial reference frame in which the measurements are made.

Geodesy is at the heart of all present day ocean studies. It is the means by which the orbit reference is computed for analysis of all satellite altimeter data, and the accuracy with which that data can be tied to tide gauge and other data. Knowledge of the reference frame geo-center and its change are needed to study regional and global sea level change and ocean-climate cycles like El Niño, the North Atlantic Oscillation, and the Pacific Decadal Oscillation.

![](/images/science1_300.jpg)

Space Geodesy provides positioning, navigation, and timing reference systems and Earth system observations

Geodesy contributes to atmospheric science and hydrological studies. It supports both observation and prediction of the weather by (1) geo-referencing meteorological observing data, (2) providing atmospheric weather models with space- and time-varying gravity fields, and (3) globally tracking change in stratospheric mass and lower tropospheric water vapor fields. Geodesy also provides a unique global reference system for measurements of: sub-seasonal, seasonal and secular movements of continental and basin-scale water masses; the wet part of the troposphere from atmospheric sounding; loading and un-loading of the land surface due to seasonal changes of groundwater; local measurement of the integral variation of ground water from permanent gravimetric tidal stations; measurement of water level of major lakes and rivers by satellite altimetry; improved digital terrain models, as basis for flux modeling of surface water and flood modeling; geographic information systems for the establishment of comprehensive referenced data modeling.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Geodesy is the scientific discipline concerned with accurately measuring the Earth's geometric shape, orientation in space, and gravity field, essential for navigation and mapping.
Geodesy has roots tracing back to ancient civilizations, evolving from basic surveying techniques to sophisticated space-based methods, significantly enhancing our understanding of Earth.
Geodesy underpins navigation systems like GPS, providing precise location data that is crucial for transportation, aviation, maritime operations, and everyday navigation apps.
Key concepts in geodesy include geoid, reference ellipsoid, geodetic datums, and spatial reference systems, all vital for accurate positioning and mapping.
Geodesy plays a critical role in space navigation by enabling accurate tracking of spacecraft positions and movements, ensuring successful missions beyond Earth.
